instruction: What blood pressure values define gestational hypertension in pregnancy?
output: Gestational hypertension = BP >= 140/90 mmHg developing after 20 weeks, without proteinuria, resolving within 42 days postpartum. Source: Sri Lanka MOH 2013 В§H.2.

instruction: How do you diagnose pre-eclampsia?
output: Pre-eclampsia = gestational hypertension (BP >= 140/90 after 20 weeks) PLUS significant proteinuria (>= 300mg/24hr, or dipstick 2+, or protein:creatinine ratio >= 30mg/mmol). Source: MOH 2013 В§H.2 / NICE NG133.

instruction: What BP values define severe hypertension in pregnancy?
output: Severe hypertension = systolic >= 160 mmHg AND/OR diastolic >= 110 mmHg. This level requires urgent treatment to prevent maternal cerebral haemorrhage. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.2.

instruction: What are the features of severe pre-eclampsia beyond high BP and proteinuria?
output: Severe pre-eclampsia features: severe headache, visual disturbance (blurring/flashing/scotomas), epigastric or RUQ pain, nausea/vomiting, clonus >= 3 beats, papilloedema, liver tenderness, oliguria (<400ml/day or <0.5ml/kg/hr over 4hr), platelets <100,000/L, AST/ALT >70 IU/L, HELLP syndrome. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.2.

instruction: How often should blood pressure be checked in antenatal visits?
output: BP must be measured at every antenatal clinic visit by a medical officer, results recorded and plotted in the pregnancy record. Proteinuria must be tested at every visit too. Refer for specialist care if BP > 140/90 on two occasions at least 2 hours apart. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.3.

instruction: Which pregnant women should start aspirin 75mg for pre-eclampsia prevention?
output: Start aspirin 75mg daily from 12 weeks until delivery if ANY ONE high-risk factor: previous hypertensive pregnancy, chronic kidney disease, autoimmune disease (SLE/APS), type 1 or 2 diabetes, chronic hypertension, multiple pregnancy. OR if TWO+ moderate-risk: first pregnancy, age >= 40, pregnancy interval > 10 years, BMI >= 35 at first visit, family history of pre-eclampsia. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.4 / NICE NG133.

instruction: What is the recommended calcium supplementation for pre-eclampsia prevention in Sri Lanka?
output: Women with risk factors should aim for daily calcium intake >= 1000mg. Given typical Sri Lankan dietary intake, the recommended supplementation is 600mg/day. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.4.

instruction: 32-year-old G3P2, 34 weeks, BP 162/108, proteinuria 2+, severe headache. What is the immediate action?
output: URGENT вҖ” this is severe pre-eclampsia with imminent eclampsia signs (severe headache + severe BP). Actions: (1) Establish IV access. (2) MgSO4 4g IV loading dose over 10 min, then 1g/hr infusion to prevent eclampsia. (3) Labetalol 20mg IV over 2 min (or oral nifedipine 10mg if no IV access) вҖ” target BP < 160/110. (4) Urgent referral to specialist obstetric unit. (5) Monitor reflexes, RR, urine output. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.1, В§6.2.2.

instruction: Pregnant woman at 28 weeks with BP 145/92, no proteinuria, no symptoms. What do I do?
output: MEDIUM вҖ” gestational hypertension. Action: (1) Recheck BP after rest in left-lateral position. (2) Dipstick urine for protein. (3) If confirmed BP 140-159/90-109 without proteinuria and asymptomatic, refer to specialist clinic for shared care. (4) Schedule weekly BP and urine checks. (5) Teach mother the danger signs (severe headache, visual disturbance, epigastric pain, reduced fetal movement). Source: MOH 2013 В§H.3, В§5.

instruction: Pregnant woman with BP 178/115, no other symptoms. Specialist hospital is 2 hours away. What do I do before transfer?
output: URGENT вҖ” severe hypertension. Pre-transfer actions: (1) IV access. (2) Oral nifedipine 10mg (repeat every 20 min, max 40mg) to bring BP < 160/110. (3) MgSO4 4g IV (or IM 5g each buttock) loading dose to prevent eclampsia during transit. (4) Insert Foley catheter and monitor urine output. (5) Call receiving hospital and brief them. (6) Send accompanied by a staff member with emergency drugs. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.4.

instruction: What is the dose of labetalol for severe hypertension in pregnancy?
output: Labetalol options: (a) Oral 200mg stat, repeat hourly up to 4 hours (only if BP < 180/110). (b) IV 20mg over 2 min вҖ” recheck BP at 10 min; if still high give 40mg IV; max single doses 20, 40, 80 mg. (c) IV infusion 40mg/hr, double every 30 min up to max 160 mg/hr. AVOID in asthma. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.1.1.

instruction: How do I give IV hydralazine for severe hypertension in pregnancy?
output: Hydralazine 5-10mg IV bolus over 2 min, accompanied by a fluid bolus of 5ml/kg of 0.9% NaCl or Ringer lactate over 30 min started at the same time (prevents drastic hypotension). Recheck BP at 15 min. Repeat 5-10mg IV every 15 min up to max 20mg total. If no lasting effect, infusion 2 mg/hr increasing by 0.5 mg/hr (range 2-20 mg/hr). DO NOT give the fluid bolus if pulmonary oedema present. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.1.2.

instruction: Oral nifedipine for hypertension in pregnancy вҖ” dose?
output: Oral nifedipine 10mg stat (use only if BP < 180/110 and patient asymptomatic). Repeat at 20-min intervals up to max 40mg total. If no response, escalate to IV labetalol or hydralazine. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.1.3.

instruction: What is the Pritchard regimen for magnesium sulphate in eclampsia?
output: Loading dose: 4g IV over 10 min (diluted to 20ml with 0.9% NaCl) PLUS 5g IM into each buttock with 1ml of 2% lignocaine in the same syringe (total 14g). Maintenance: 5g IM every 4 hours, alternating buttocks, with lignocaine. Continue for 24 hours after delivery or last fit, whichever is later. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.2.

instruction: What is the Zuspan regimen for magnesium sulphate?
output: Loading dose: 4g IV over 10 min, diluted to 20ml with 0.9% NaCl (via pump) or 80ml via burette. Maintenance: 1g/hr IV infusion. Continue for 24 hours after delivery or last fit. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.2.

instruction: What monitoring is required for a woman receiving magnesium sulphate?
output: Monitor and record every 30 min: (1) Hourly urine output (target >= 30 ml/hr). (2) Respiratory rate (target > 16/min). (3) Oxygen saturation (target > 90%). (4) Presence of patellar reflexes. Stop MgSO4 if RR < 12, absent reflexes, or oliguria. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.2.

instruction: Signs of magnesium sulphate toxicity and the antidote?
output: Toxicity signs: respiratory depression (RR < 12), loss of patellar reflexes, oliguria, drowsiness. Antidote: calcium gluconate 1g IV (10ml of 10% solution) over 10 min. STOP MgSO4 infusion immediately. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.2.

instruction: A woman has just had a generalized seizure at 33 weeks with BP 168/112. Immediate action?
output: URGENT вҖ” eclampsia. During seizure: (1) Turn to LEFT lateral position. (2) Clear airway, suction secretions, O2 by face mask 8-10 L/min. (3) Protect from injury. After seizure: (4) IV access вҖ” bloods for FBC, LFT, U&E, coagulation, cross-match. (5) MgSO4 4g IV bolus over 10 min, then 1g/hr infusion. (6) Control BP < 160/110 with labetalol/hydralazine. (7) Insert Foley catheter, restrict fluids to 80 ml/hr. (8) Plan delivery once stable. Source: MOH 2013 В§I.6.

instruction: What do I do if a woman has a second seizure while on magnesium sulphate?
output: About 10% of women on MgSO4 will have a second seizure. Give additional MgSO4 2g IV (diluted to 10ml with NaCl, over 5 min) AND increase the infusion to 2g/hr. Monitor closely. If further seizures, call neurology/medical team, consider second-line anticonvulsants in ICU. Source: MOH 2013 В§I.6.4.

instruction: Total fluid intake limit for a woman with severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia?
output: Restrict total fluids to 80 ml/hr to prevent pulmonary oedema. Accurate fluid balance is essential. Use infusion pumps. Account for all drug volumes. Avoid NSAIDs until fluid status recovers. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.3.

instruction: Is eclampsia an indication for caesarean section?
output: NO вҖ” eclampsia itself is not an indication for CS. Decision depends on cervix and fetal/maternal condition. If cervix is unfavourable (Bishop < 7) and the woman is not in labour, CS may be appropriate. If in labour or favourable cervix, vaginal delivery is preferred. Stabilise the mother first; never deliver an unstable mother for fetal reasons. Source: MOH 2013 В§I.7.

instruction: Is ergometrine safe to give for the third stage of labour in severe pre-eclampsia?
output: NO. Ergometrine is contraindicated in severe pre-eclampsia/eclampsia вҖ” it causes acute rises in BP that can precipitate stroke. Use oxytocin 10 IU IM (or 5 IU IV) instead for active management of the third stage. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.5.

instruction: What is HELLP syndrome and how is it diagnosed?
output: HELLP = Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets вҖ” a severe pre-eclampsia variant. Diagnosis: AST or ALT > 70 IU/L AND platelets < 100,000/L AND evidence of haemolysis (raised LDH, low haptoglobin, schistocytes on blood film). Action: treat as severe pre-eclampsia, plan delivery once stable. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.2.

instruction: When should magnesium sulphate be discontinued postpartum?
output: Continue MgSO4 for at least 24 hours AFTER delivery, or 24 hours after the last seizure вҖ” whichever is later. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.6.

instruction: A woman had severe pre-eclampsia in this pregnancy. What is her risk in a future pregnancy?
output: Counselling: risk of gestational hypertension ~53% (1 in 2); risk of pre-eclampsia ~16% (1 in 6). If she had severe hypertension/HELLP/eclampsia OR delivered before 34 weeks, recurrence risk of pre-eclampsia is ~25%; if delivered before 28 weeks, ~55%. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.7.

instruction: Which antihypertensive should be avoided postpartum?
output: Methyldopa вҖ” its tendency to cause depression makes it best avoided postpartum. Switch to another agent (e.g. labetalol, nifedipine) on discharge. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.6.2.6.

instruction: Mother on antihypertensive medication is planning pregnancy. Which drugs must I stop?
output: Stop ACE inhibitors (e.g. enalapril, captopril), ARBs (e.g. losartan), and statins BEFORE conception вҖ” they are teratogenic. Switch to labetalol, methyldopa, or nifedipine. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.5 / RCOG.

instruction: What is the upper limit of normal BP at 12 weeks gestation if she has a history of chronic hypertension?
output: In chronic hypertension, target BP during pregnancy is around 130-140/90-100. The same severe-hypertension threshold applies: treat aggressively if BP >= 160/110 or symptoms. Anticipate superimposed pre-eclampsia. Source: MOH 2013 В§H.5.

instruction: What is the definition of primary postpartum haemorrhage?
output: Primary PPH = blood loss >= 500 ml from the genital tract within 24 hours of birth. Major PPH = blood loss >= 1000 ml. Regardless of volume, any cardiovascular instability (tachycardia + hypotension) signifies major obstetric haemorrhage. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.2.

instruction: How do I estimate blood volume in a 60 kg pregnant woman?
output: Blood volume = body weight (kg) / 12. So 60 kg / 12 = 5 litres. Loss of >= 40% of blood volume (~2400 ml in a 60 kg woman) is a massive obstetric haemorrhage. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.2.

instruction: List the risk factors for PPH that I should look for during antenatal care.
output: PPH risk factors: grand multiparity, previous PPH, fibroids, anaemia, pre-existing bleeding disorders, anticoagulant use, obesity, pre-eclampsia/gestational hypertension, uterine over-distension (twins, polyhydramnios), large baby (>4 kg), chorioamnionitis, dengue infection. Women with risk factors should deliver in a specialist obstetric unit with IV access secured (14-16 G) and blood group known. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.4.

instruction: What are the steps of active management of the third stage of labour?
output: Active management of third stage: (1) Oxytocin 5 IU IV slowly OR 10 IU IM right after delivery of the baby. (2) Delayed cord clamping for 2 min (unless baby compromised, Rh-iso, or maternal bleeding). (3) Controlled cord traction with counter-traction above symphysis pubis. (4) Uterine massage after delivery of placenta. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.3.2.3.

instruction: After delivery, the uterus feels soft and there is continuing fresh bleeding. What is the first step?
output: Atonic PPH. Steps in order: (1) Call for help. (2) Rub up the fundus (uterine massage). (3) Clear cervix and vagina of clots by VE. (4) Ergometrine 0.5mg slow IV (or methylergometrine 0.2mg) OR oxytocin 5 IU IV + start infusion 40 IU in 500 ml Hartmann at 125 ml/hr. (5) Bimanual compression. (6) If still bleeding after 10 min, repeat ergometrine. (7) Misoprostol 800 micrograms PR or SL. (8) Tranexamic acid 1g IV over 10 min (repeat at 30 min if needed). (9) Balloon tamponade. (10) Inform Consultant. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.5.2.2.

instruction: How do I do a uterine balloon tamponade with a Foley catheter and condom?
output: Condom catheter tamponade: (1) Size 22 Foley catheter, sterile condom, No. 0/1 suture, warm saline. (2) Tie the condom firmly over the catheter 2 cm from the open end. (3) Test with saline to confirm watertight. (4) Insert the catheter through the cervix into the uterus. (5) Fill with 400-500 ml warm saline by gravity until bleeding stops or the balloon bulges out of the cervix. (6) Pack the vagina with moist ribbon gauze around the catheter. (7) Insert Foley catheter into bladder. (8) Give tranexamic acid 1g IV + antibiotic prophylaxis. (9) Keep for 12-18 hours. (10) Deflate half the volume, observe 30 min, then deflate fully and remove. Source: MOH 2013 В§K Appendix 1.

instruction: When is hysterectomy indicated in PPH?
output: Hysterectomy is indicated when all conservative measures fail вҖ” uterine atony unresponsive to drugs + balloon tamponade + brace sutures + uterine devascularisation. KEY MESSAGE: too little, too late kills women. Do not delay hysterectomy once it is clear the uterus cannot be salvaged. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.5.2.2.

instruction: Is misoprostol licensed for PPH in Sri Lanka?
output: Misoprostol is not formally licensed in Sri Lanka for induction or PPH at the time of the MOH 2013 guideline, but it is recommended for use in PPH (800 mcg PR or SL) when ergometrine and oxytocin have failed вҖ” its effectiveness in this setting is well-established. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.5.2.2.

instruction: Tranexamic acid dose for PPH?
output: Tranexamic acid 1g IV slowly over 10 min. May be repeated after 30 min if bleeding continues. WOMAN trial evidence: most effective if given within 3 hours of bleeding onset. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.5.2.2 / WOMAN trial.

instruction: I see continuing fresh bleeding but the uterus is well contracted. What do I do?
output: Suspect traumatic PPH вҖ” genital tract trauma. Action: (1) Place in lithotomy under good light and analgesia. (2) Inspect upper vagina and cervix systematically with Green-Armytage forceps. (3) Examine for paravaginal and broad ligament haematoma by combined PV and PR exam. (4) Repair tears starting above the apex. (5) For haematoma > 5 cm, surgical evacuation. (6) If multiple oozing tears, consider balloon catheter in vagina or moistened vaginal pack. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.5.2.3.

instruction: A woman delivered 30 min ago, BP 80/50, pulse 130, pale, sweaty, fundus high above umbilicus. Diagnosis?
output: URGENT вҖ” concealed PPH (probably uterine atony with clot retention) OR uterine rupture. Action: (1) Two large-bore IVs (14-16 G). (2) Cross-match 4 units, full bloods + coagulation. (3) Fluid resuscitation вҖ” warm crystalloid up to 2L + colloid up to 1-2L until blood arrives. (4) Rub up fundus, clear clots from cervix. (5) Oxytocin infusion + ergometrine + tranexamic acid. (6) Call Consultant immediately вҖ” laparotomy may be needed if rupture. Keep her flat and warm. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.5.2.4.

instruction: Should I give Hartmann's solution or normal saline for PPH resuscitation?
output: Either is acceptable. MOH recommends warmed crystalloid up to 2 litres + colloid up to 1-2 litres (max 3.5 L combined) until blood is available. Hartmann's (Ringer lactate) is often preferred over normal saline to avoid hyperchloraemic acidosis. Keep fluids warm вҖ” hypothermia worsens coagulopathy. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.6.

instruction: How do I prevent hypothermia during a PPH?
output: Pay attention to temperature of labour/theatre room, IV fluids, blood products, and lavage fluids вҖ” all should be warmed. Cover the woman with warm blankets. Hypothermia promotes coagulopathy and worsens outcome. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.5.1.

instruction: What is delayed third stage of labour and how do I manage it?
output: Delayed third stage = placenta not delivered within 30 min of active management. Action: (1) Intraumbilical vein oxytocin 50 IU in 30 ml of 0.9% NaCl. (2) Wait 30 min, attempt controlled cord traction again. (3) If still not delivered, proceed to manual removal of placenta under anaesthesia. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.3.2.3.2.

instruction: When should blood transfusion start in PPH?
output: Transfuse warm blood as soon as available for blood loss > 1000 ml or earlier if cardiovascular instability. Until blood is available, give warm crystalloid up to 2L + colloid up to 1-2L. Group-specific blood is acceptable until cross-matched is ready. If neither, give O Rhesus D negative. For >= 3 units of blood, transfuse fresh frozen plasma in equal amount. Source: MOH 2013 В§K.6.

instruction: What is a B-Lynch suture and when is it indicated?
output: B-Lynch (brace) suture is a uterine compression suture for atonic PPH unresponsive to medical management. A No. 2 absorbable suture is passed antero-posteriorly through the uterus on each side, tied over the fundus while an assistant compresses the uterus manually. May be combined with a balloon tamponade ('sandwich technique'). Source: MOH 2013 В§K Appendix 2.

instruction: What is acute uterine inversion and how do I recognize it?
output: Acute puerperal inversion = the uterine fundus turns inside out into the cavity, usually due to cord traction on a relaxed uterus. RED FLAGS: shock out of proportion to visible blood loss, sudden severe lower abdominal pain in 3rd stage, fundus absent or 'cupped' on abdominal palpation, vaginal mass visible/palpable. PV exam shows mass with cervical ring around its base. Source: MOH 2013 В§G.6.

instruction: Immediate management of acute uterine inversion?
output: URGENT вҖ” life-threatening. (1) Call for help, treat shock aggressively (note neurogenic component). (2) Do NOT remove the placenta if still attached. (3) Reposition uterus IMMEDIATELY (sooner is easier) вҖ” Johnson manoeuvre: hand inside vagina, lift fundus above umbilicus to dilate the cervical ring. (4) Hold in place after reduction; give oxytocin 5-10 IU IV + infusion. (5) If unable in labour room, GA and theatre. (6) Hydrostatic O'Sullivan reduction with warm saline if manual fails. Source: MOH 2013 В§G.7.

instruction: When should pregnant women be screened for diabetes in Sri Lanka?
output: Universal screening of ALL Sri Lankan pregnant women is recommended at the first visit (ideally < 12 weeks) and again at 24-28 weeks if first test negative. Sri Lankans are at high risk (South Asian + pregnant). Already-known diabetics are not re-screened вҖ” start glycaemic control immediately. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.2.1.

instruction: What is the DIPSI test and how do I do it?
output: DIPSI = Diabetes in Pregnancy Study Group of India test вҖ” a non-fasting 75g OGCT. Give 75g glucose dissolved in 300ml water (with lime juice for taste) regardless of fasting status, over 3-5 min. Measure plasma glucose at 2 hours. A 2-hr value > 140 mg/dl (7.8 mmol/L) confirms GDM. Advantage: no fasting required, single sample, universal applicability. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.2.2.

instruction: What is the diagnostic threshold for pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?
output: Pre-existing diabetes if ANY one of: FBS >= 126 mg/dl (7.0 mmol/L), random BG > 200 mg/dl (11.1 mmol/L), or HbA1c > 6.1%. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.2.1.

instruction: Which tests should NOT be used to screen for GDM?
output: Do NOT use: fasting blood glucose, random blood glucose, 50g glucose challenge test, HbA1c, or urinalysis for reducing substances вҖ” these are NOT validated for GDM screening in pregnancy. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.2.1.

instruction: Target blood glucose values for pregnant women with diabetes?
output: Targets (venous plasma): fasting/pre-meal 70-90 mg/dl (3.9-5.0 mmol/L); 2-hr post-meal < 120 mg/dl (6.7 mmol/L). Capillary equivalents: fasting 80-103 mg/dl, post-meal < 118 mg/dl. Aim for these without causing hypoglycaemia. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.4.1.

instruction: Pre-pregnancy HbA1c target for known diabetic women planning pregnancy?
output: Target HbA1c < 6.1% before conception. Women with HbA1c > 10% should be strongly advised against conception until control improves вҖ” risk of congenital malformations rises sharply. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.3.1.

instruction: Folic acid dose for women with diabetes planning pregnancy?
output: 5mg folic acid daily when trying to conceive, continuing until 12 weeks gestation. After 12 weeks, reduce to 1mg daily. Higher dose is to reduce neural tube defect risk, which is elevated in diabetes. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.3.1, В§J.3.2.

instruction: Which diabetes drugs are safe in pregnancy?
output: Safe in pregnancy: insulin (all preparations), metformin. STOP before/during pregnancy: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, statins (teratogenic). Other oral hypoglycaemics вҖ” not used; switch to insulin or metformin. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.3.1, В§J.4.1.

instruction: Insulin regimen for a newly-diagnosed GDM woman not controlled on diet?
output: Two main options: (1) Three pre-meal short-acting + bedtime basal insulin (best control for moderate-severe hyperglycaemia). (2) Twice-daily pre-mixed 30:70 insulin (high compliance, adequate for most cases). If twice-daily insufficient, add metformin or a soluble insulin to cover lunch. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.4.1.

instruction: How should I monitor fetal growth in a diabetic pregnancy?
output: Ultrasound abdominal circumference (AC) at 28, 32, 36 weeks. If AC > 90th centile at any stage, intensify insulin to target 2-hr PPBS < 100 mg/dl (avoiding hypoglycaemia). If AC < 10th centile or crossing centiles down, do amniotic fluid index and refer for obstetric review. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.4.2.

instruction: Why is HbA1c unreliable in late pregnancy?
output: HbA1c is NOT reliable in the second and third trimesters because of increased red cell turnover and the dilutional effect of pregnancy. Rely on self-monitored blood glucose (SMBG) or regular 6-point profiles instead. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.4.2.

instruction: When should a woman with diabetes deliver?
output: Pre-existing diabetes or insulin-treated GDM: schedule delivery at 38-39 weeks (obstetrician review at 36-37 weeks). Diet-controlled GDM with optimal glycaemia and normally-grown baby: insufficient evidence for a specific timing вҖ” individualize. Diabetes alone is NOT an indication for caesarean section. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.5.1.

instruction: Blood glucose target during labour in a diabetic woman?
output: Maintain capillary blood glucose between 4 and 7 mmol/L (72-126 mg/dl) during labour. Check 1-2 hourly. Record on the partogram. Start Hartmann's/saline if low; insulin-dextrose-potassium (GIK) infusion if high. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.5.2.

instruction: Newborn of a diabetic mother вҖ” when do I check blood sugar?
output: Check newborn capillary blood glucose 2-4 hours after birth. Action threshold: < 36 mg/dl (2 mmol/L). Initiate breastfeeding within 30-60 min to prevent hypoglycaemia. Watch for polycythaemia, hyperbilirubinaemia, hypocalcaemia, hypomagnesaemia in clinical signs. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.6.1.a.

instruction: When should a woman who had GDM be re-tested for diabetes after delivery?
output: At 6-8 weeks postpartum, repeat 75g OGTT (ideal) or fasting BG. If negative, lifestyle advice + annual FBS thereafter. Encourage breastfeeding (contributes to weight loss and reduces type 2 DM risk). Aim for normal BMI. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.6.3.

instruction: Which contraceptive should be avoided in a postnatal woman with BMI > 25 and GDM?
output: Avoid DMPA (Depot Medroxy Progesterone Acetate) вҖ” associated with weight gain in women already overweight. All other reliable methods (COCP, POP, IUD, condoms) are acceptable based on individual needs. Women with type 2 DM should be advised to complete their family within 5-10 years of diagnosis. Source: MOH 2013 В§J.7.

instruction: How do I diagnose labour?
output: Labour = regular, painful intermittent contractions of increasing frequency, duration, and intensity, leading to progressive cervical effacement and dilatation. For practical purposes, also diagnose labour with painful contractions occurring at >= 2 in 10 minutes. If uncertain, observe and reassess in 4 hours. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.2.

instruction: What is the latent phase of labour?
output: Latent phase = from commencement of labour to cervical dilatation of 4 cm. There are painful contractions and some cervical changes including effacement and dilatation up to 4 cm. This phase is best managed in the antenatal ward, not the labour room. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.2, В§A.3.2.1.1.

instruction: When is the latent phase considered prolonged?
output: Latent phase is prolonged when it lasts > 12 hours in a primigravida OR > 8 hours in a multigravida. In these cases, an experienced medical officer must reassess with a view to augmentation. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.3.2.1.1.

instruction: What defines delayed progress in the active phase of first stage?
output: Delayed progress = cervical dilatation of < 2 cm in 4 hours, OR slowing of progress in a woman previously progressing satisfactorily. This MUST be assessed by an experienced medical officer. Consider: uterine contractions, descent and position of fetal head, signs of obstruction (caput, moulding), and fetal condition. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.3.2.1.3.

instruction: Time limit for the active second stage of labour?
output: Primigravida: birth expected within 2 hours of start of active second stage; diagnose delay at 1 hour and seek advice from a trained operator. Multigravida: birth expected within 1 hour; diagnose delay at 30 min. Add 1 hour to each if epidural in situ. Delay in a multipara raises strong suspicion of disproportion or malposition. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.3.2.2.2.

instruction: When should I do continuous electronic fetal monitoring rather than intermittent auscultation?
output: Continuous EFM (CTG) is indicated for: significant meconium staining of amniotic fluid; abnormal FHR by intermittent auscultation (< 110 or > 160 bpm, decelerations after contractions); fresh vaginal bleeding; maternal pyrexia; oxytocin use for augmentation/induction; scarred uterus; epidural analgesia; growth-restricted baby. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.3.2.1.2, В§D.

instruction: What is a normal FHR baseline in a term fetus?
output: Normal FHR baseline: 110-160 bpm. Auscultate immediately after a contraction for at least 1 full minute. Always palpate the maternal pulse to differentiate from fetal heart rate. Source: MOH 2013 В§D.

instruction: Classify a CTG with baseline 165 bpm, variability 8 bpm, no decelerations, accelerations present.
output: REASSURING in 3/4 features; baseline 161-180 is non-reassuring. Overall classification: SUSPICIOUS (one non-reassuring feature). Action: continue EFM, review by experienced medical officer, look for reversible causes (maternal fever, dehydration, recent drugs). If features evolve or persist > 90 min, escalate. Source: MOH 2013 В§D Table 1, Table 2.

instruction: Define pathological CTG.
output: Pathological CTG = two or more non-reassuring features OR one or more abnormal features. Abnormal features: baseline < 100 or > 180, sinusoidal pattern >= 10 min, variability < 5 for 90 min, atypical variable decelerations affecting > 50% of contractions for > 30 min, or late decelerations for > 30 min. Action: urgent obstetric review, prepare for delivery. Source: MOH 2013 В§D Table 1, Table 2.

instruction: What is the partogram alert line and action line?
output: Alert line: drawn at 1 cm per hour starting from 4 cm dilatation. Action line: parallel to the alert line, 4 hours to the right. Crossing the alert line prompts reassessment; crossing the action line mandates active intervention (augmentation, surgical decision). Source: MOH 2013 В§F (partogram).

instruction: When is amniotomy contraindicated?
output: Amniotomy is contraindicated when the head is high and poorly applied to the cervix (cord prolapse risk), and in cases of suspected vasa praevia or placenta praevia. Only do amniotomy when committed to delivery within 24 hours, when the cervix is ripe, and after assessment by an experienced clinician. Source: MOH 2013 В§B.6.2.

instruction: Indications for induction of labour at 41 weeks in a low-risk pregnancy?
output: Induction is recommended for low-risk women with confirmed gestation at 41 weeks. Around 40 weeks, assess fetal wellbeing (biometry вҖ” at least AC вҖ” and amniotic fluid index; lower cut-off AFI 7 cm) to select those for conservative management until 41 weeks. Source: MOH 2013 В§B.4.1.

instruction: How do I induce labour with intrauterine death?
output: Intrauterine death is a traumatic event вҖ” most women want delivery soon and their wishes should be respected. Prostaglandins are preferred. Amniotomy and repeated VEs should be AVOIDED to reduce infection risk. (Misoprostol is highly effective in mid-trimester IUD; mifepristone helps but neither is currently licensed in Sri Lanka.) Amniotomy preferred only with abruption placentae. Source: MOH 2013 В§B.4.4.

instruction: What is the recommended oxytocin starting infusion rate?
output: Standard mix: 5 IU oxytocin in 500 ml of 0.9% NaCl. Start at 15 drops/min (~7.5 mU/min), increase by 15 drops/min every 30 min, max 60 drops/min (~30 mU/min). Aim for a contraction-free interval of 2 minutes. Use an infusion pump where available. Source: MOH 2013 В§C.

instruction: Definition of uterine hyperstimulation?
output: Hyperstimulation = contraction-free interval < 60 seconds OR contractions lasting > 90 seconds. Action: STOP oxytocin (or remove PGE2 tablet from vagina) immediately, rapid 0.9% NaCl bolus via fresh giving set, consider tocolytic (terbutaline 250 mcg IV/SC; salbutamol inhaler if terbutaline unavailable). Source: MOH 2013 В§B.8.1, В§C.

instruction: When is oxytocin combined use with prostaglandins acceptable?
output: Combined use is DANGEROUS вҖ” risk of hyperstimulation, uterine rupture. Allow at least 6 hours between the last vaginal PGE2 dose and starting oxytocin. Source: MOH 2013 В§B.6.3.2.

instruction: Pethidine dose for pain relief in labour?
output: Pethidine 1-1.5 mg/kg IM, may repeat after 4-6 hours. Third dose only with senior agreement. Avoid if delivery anticipated within 4 hours. Co-administer with metoclopramide 5mg IV or 10mg IM to reduce nausea and gastric stasis. Naloxone 100 mcg/kg IV available for neonatal respiratory depression. Source: MOH 2013 В§E.1.2.2.A.

instruction: What is the routine episiotomy policy?
output: Routine episiotomy should NOT be carried out during spontaneous vaginal birth. Episiotomy is selective вҖ” for instrumental birth, suspected fetal compromise, or imminent severe perineal tearing. When indicated, perform mediolateral episiotomy at 45-60 degrees to the right, beginning at the fourchette, at the time of crowning, after infiltration with up to 20ml of 1% lignocaine. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.3.2.2.5.

instruction: How do I classify perineal tears?
output: First degree: skin only. Second degree: perineal muscles, not anal sphincter. Third degree: involves anal sphincter complex. Fourth degree: involves anal sphincter complex AND anal epithelium. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.5.

instruction: Preferred suture material for perineal repair?
output: Rapidly absorbable polyglactin acid (e.g. Vicryl Rapide). Repair under tested effective analgesia вҖ” infiltration with up to 20ml of 1% lignocaine or by topping up an epidural. Always do a rectal examination after repair to ensure no sutures through rectal mucosa. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.5.

instruction: When should breastfeeding be initiated?
output: Initiate breastfeeding within 30 minutes of birth (ideally within 1 hour). Deliver baby onto mother's abdomen, dry, skin-to-skin, then put baby between mother's breasts for skin-to-skin care. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.4.

instruction: Should I always do a vaginal examination on admission to labour room?
output: VE should be OFFERED, not mandatory. Before VE: ensure valid indication, consent, privacy, dignity, comfort; explain procedure and findings sensitively. VE is invasive and can be distressing вҖ” only do it if it will add to decision-making. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.3.2.1.2a.

instruction: What food and drink is allowed in labour?
output: Clear non-fizzy liquids are encouraged throughout labour (oral rehydration fluid or king coconut water are better than plain water вҖ” isotonic). In the LATENT phase, light solids like biscuits and fruits are also acceptable. No solids in active phase due to risk if GA needed. Source: MOH 2013 В§A.3.1.5.

instruction: What is the recommended antenatal visit schedule for a low-risk Sri Lankan pregnancy?
output: Sri Lanka uses a tailored schedule (PHM-led): booking visit before 12 weeks, then ~4-weekly until 28 weeks, 2-weekly from 28-36 weeks, weekly from 36 weeks until delivery. High-risk pregnancies have additional visits. Total minimum 8 contacts per WHO 2016 ANC recommendation. Source: WHO ANC 2016 / Sri Lanka Family Health Bureau.

instruction: Which danger signs should every pregnant mother be taught to recognize?
output: Teach all mothers to come to hospital immediately for: severe headache, blurred vision or seeing spots, swelling of face/hands, severe upper abdominal (RUQ) pain, convulsions, vaginal bleeding any amount, watery discharge before term (PROM), reduced or absent fetal movement, severe vomiting, fever, painful urination, breathlessness at rest. Source: WHO ANC / RCOG / MOH PHM training materials.

instruction: When should fetal movement counting begin and what is the threshold?
output: Fetal movement awareness from 28 weeks. No single 'count' is mandated by latest RCOG вҖ” mothers should be alert to changes in their baby's usual pattern. Reduced fetal movement = come for assessment SAME DAY. Absent fetal movement > 12 hours = URGENT. Source: RCOG GTG 57 / MOH.

instruction: When should anti-D be given to an Rh-negative pregnant woman?
output: Anti-D Ig 1500 IU IM at 28-30 weeks (single dose) OR 500 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks (two-dose regimen). Additionally for sensitizing events (bleeding, ECV, amniocentesis, miscarriage, abdominal trauma). Postpartum: within 72 hours if baby is Rh-positive. Source: RCOG GTG 22.

instruction: How much iron and folate should a pregnant woman receive routinely in Sri Lanka?
output: Sri Lanka Family Health Bureau standard: ferrous sulphate 200mg (containing 60mg elemental iron) + folic acid 400 mcg daily, started from first ANC contact, continued until 6 weeks postpartum. Calcium 600mg daily for high-risk women. Source: Sri Lanka FHB Maternal Nutrition Policy.

instruction: Which infections should every pregnant woman in Sri Lanka be screened for?
output: Routine screening at booking: HIV (with informed consent), syphilis (VDRL or RPR), Hepatitis B surface antigen, rubella IgG (if not previously immune), urine for asymptomatic bacteriuria (mid-stream culture). Plus FBC for anaemia and Hb electrophoresis if thalassaemia suspected (high prevalence in Sri Lanka). Source: Sri Lanka FHB ANC guideline.

instruction: When should tetanus toxoid be given in pregnancy?
output: Sri Lanka policy: 2 doses of Td (tetanus + diphtheria) in the first pregnancy, given 4 weeks apart, with second dose at least 4 weeks before delivery. Booster in subsequent pregnancies. Protects mother and prevents neonatal tetanus. Source: Sri Lanka FHB EPI schedule.

instruction: Which ultrasound scans are recommended in pregnancy?
output: Dating scan 7-13 weeks (best at 11-13+6 weeks with NT measurement). Anomaly scan at 18-20+6 weeks. Growth scan at 28 weeks (selective) and 36 weeks (selective). For high-risk: serial growth scans 2-4 weekly. Source: RCOG/NICE.

instruction: Reduced fetal movement at 32 weeks вҖ” what do I do?
output: Reduced fetal movement at >= 28 weeks: (1) Maternal palpation of abdomen and uterus. (2) Auscultation/CTG for 20 min minimum. (3) Same-day USS if persistent reduced movements, growth assessment, amniotic fluid volume, biophysical profile. (4) Refer for senior review. NEVER reassure on intermittent auscultation alone after 28 weeks. Source: RCOG GTG 57.

instruction: Bleeding in second/third trimester вҖ” what do I check first?
output: Antepartum haemorrhage. (1) NEVER do a digital VE before excluding placenta praevia (USS first). (2) Vital signs, fetal heart, fundal height. (3) Speculum exam to localize bleeding (cervix vs higher). (4) USS вҖ” placental site, fluid, abruption signs. (5) Cross-match, FBC, coagulation, Kleihauer if Rh-negative. (6) Refer urgently. Source: RCOG GTG 63.

instruction: What Hb defines anaemia in pregnancy in Sri Lanka?
output: Hb < 11 g/dL in the first trimester (and >= 28 weeks) OR Hb < 10.5 g/dL in the second trimester. WHO classification: 9-10.9 mild, 7-8.9 moderate, < 7 severe. (Sri Lanka MOH uses < 8 for severe/URGENT). Source: WHO + MOH 2013.

instruction: What is the iron dose for treating iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy?
output: Therapeutic dose: oral elemental iron 100-200 mg/day (e.g. ferrous sulphate 200mg tid). Take between meals with vitamin C (orange juice) for absorption. Avoid taking with tea/coffee/calcium. Recheck Hb in 2 weeks вҖ” rise of 1 g/dL expected. Source: RCOG GTG 47 / Sri Lanka FHB.

instruction: When should I consider IV iron in pregnancy?
output: IV iron (iron sucrose or ferric carboxymaltose) is indicated when: oral iron not tolerated (severe GI side effects), absorption impaired, non-compliance demonstrated, Hb < 9 g/dL after 34 weeks (no time for oral to work), or non-response after 2-4 weeks of oral therapy. Source: RCOG GTG 47.

instruction: When should I transfuse blood for anaemia in pregnancy?
output: Transfusion thresholds (no active bleeding): Hb < 7 g/dL with cardiac/respiratory symptoms, Hb < 6 g/dL generally, or symptomatic Hb 6-8 g/dL near term. Avoid transfusion if oral/IV iron will work in time. Always document indication and obtain informed consent. Source: RCOG GTG 47.

instruction: What is the role of thalassaemia screening in Sri Lankan pregnancy?
output: Sri Lanka has high prevalence of thalassaemia (~2% beta-thalassaemia trait). Screen all women at booking with FBC + Hb electrophoresis if MCV/MCH low or family history. If mother is carrier, test partner. If both carriers, refer for prenatal diagnosis counselling. Source: Sri Lanka National Thalassaemia Programme.

instruction: Pregnant woman with Hb 7.5 g/dL at 32 weeks, no symptoms, no bleeding. Plan?
output: MODERATE anaemia вҖ” urgent action without transfusion. (1) Confirm iron deficiency: low ferritin, low MCV, low MCH; rule out thalassaemia. (2) Start oral elemental iron 200 mg/day + folic acid 5 mg + vitamin C. (3) If poor tolerance or < 4 weeks to delivery, start IV iron sucrose. (4) Re-check Hb in 2 weeks. (5) Refer to specialist for IV iron + delivery planning. Source: RCOG GTG 47 + MOH.

instruction: Hb < 8 g/dL in a pregnant woman вҖ” risk level?
output: URGENT вҖ” severe anaemia. High risk of decompensation in labour and PPH. Action: same-day specialist referral, IV iron or transfusion based on gestation, repeat Hb 48 hours, plan delivery in tertiary facility with blood available. Source: MOH 2013 / RCOG GTG 47.

instruction: Breech presentation at 36 weeks вҖ” what do I do?
output: (1) Confirm by USS. (2) Counsel mother about external cephalic version (ECV) вҖ” success rate 50-60% in nulliparas, higher in multiparas. (3) Offer ECV at 36-37 weeks. (4) Contraindications to ECV: placenta praevia, ruptured membranes, abnormal CTG, multiple pregnancy, recent APH, scarred uterus (relative). (5) If ECV fails or declined: discuss planned CS vs vaginal breech birth (CS recommended in most settings). Source: RCOG GTG 20a, 20b.

instruction: VBAC вҖ” what are the success rates and major risks?
output: Vaginal birth after caesarean: success rate ~72-75% overall. Major risk: uterine scar rupture ~0.5% with one previous CS, increases with multiple previous CS or short inter-pregnancy interval. Counselling required. Suitable if: one previous lower-segment CS, no other CS indication this pregnancy, no contraindication to vaginal birth. Continuous CTG in labour. Avoid prostaglandins for IOL. Source: RCOG GTG 45.

instruction: Group B Strep screening policy?
output: Sri Lanka does not have universal GBS screening. Use risk-based approach: intrapartum IV penicillin if previous baby with GBS disease, GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy, preterm labour < 37 weeks, prolonged ROM > 18 hours, intrapartum fever >= 38 C. Source: RCOG GTG 36 (risk-based) / UK NICE.

instruction: Twin pregnancy вҖ” what extra care is needed?
output: (1) Confirm chorionicity at first scan (11-13+6 weeks) вҖ” best by lambda/T sign. (2) DCDA: scans every 4 weeks from 20 weeks. MCDA: 2-weekly scans from 16 weeks (TTTS surveillance). MCMA: weekly from 24 weeks. (3) Aspirin 75-150 mg from 12 weeks (pre-eclampsia prevention). (4) Iron and folate routine. (5) Plan delivery: uncomplicated DCDA 37+0-37+6, MCDA 36+0-36+6, MCMA 32-34 weeks by CS. Source: RCOG/NICE NG137.

instruction: Suspected chorioamnionitis in labour вҖ” action?
output: Maternal fever >= 38 C, fetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, foul-smelling liquor. (1) IV broad-spectrum antibiotics вҖ” typically ampicillin 2g IV q6h + gentamicin 5mg/kg IV od (cover GBS, E. coli, anaerobes). (2) Continuous fetal monitoring. (3) Plan delivery вҖ” do not delay. (4) Alert neonatal team. Source: RCOG / Sri Lanka MOH antibiotic policy.

instruction: Dengue in pregnancy вҖ” special considerations?
output: Dengue is common in Sri Lanka and a PPH risk factor. (1) Daily FBC during febrile phase вҖ” thrombocytopenia and haemoconcentration. (2) Watch for warning signs of DHF (severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, restlessness, bleeding). (3) Fluid management critical вҖ” avoid both over and under-resuscitation. (4) Delivery during shock phase is high risk вҖ” try to defer until recovery if possible. (5) Cross-match blood early. Source: Sri Lanka MOH Dengue Guidelines + WHO.

instruction: Premature rupture of membranes at term (after 37 weeks) вҖ” management?
output: Term PROM. In absence of fetal or maternal compromise: (1) Watchful expectant management for up to 24 hours. (2) If no spontaneous labour in 24 hours, induce вҖ” either oxytocin infusion or prostaglandin. (3) Avoid digital VE to reduce ascending infection. (4) Speculum exam if needed. (5) Monitor for chorioamnionitis. Source: MOH 2013 В§B.4.2 / RCOG GTG 44.

instruction: Preterm PROM at 32 weeks вҖ” management?
output: PPROM at 32 weeks: (1) Speculum to confirm. (2) Avoid digital VE. (3) Vaginal/cervical swab. (4) Antibiotics вҖ” erythromycin 250mg qid for 10 days (prolongs latency). (5) Corticosteroids: dexamethasone 6mg IM q12h x 4 doses or betamethasone 12mg IM q24h x 2 doses (fetal lung maturity). (6) MgSO4 if delivery imminent < 32 weeks (neuroprotection). (7) Plan delivery after 34 weeks if no other indication earlier. Source: MOH 2013 В§B.4.3 + RCOG GTG 73.

instruction: What aspirin dose does NICE NG133 recommend for women at high risk of pre-eclampsia?
output: Aspirin 75 mg to 150 mg once daily from 12 weeks until birth of the baby. Note: dose was updated upward from the 2010 guideline (75 mg) вҖ” current NICE recommendation accepts up to 150 mg. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.1.2.

instruction: Symptoms that NICE NG133 says require IMMEDIATE review for pre-eclampsia?
output: Severe headache; problems with vision (blurring, flashing); severe pain just below the ribs (epigastric); vomiting; sudden swelling of the face, hands or feet. Advise every pregnant woman to see a healthcare professional immediately if any of these appear. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.1.1.

instruction: Should I order a 24-hour urine collection to quantify proteinuria in a pregnant woman?
output: No. NICE NG133 В§1.2.5 says: do not routinely use 24-hour urine collection to quantify proteinuria. Use protein:creatinine ratio or albumin:creatinine ratio on a spot sample instead (threshold 30 mg/mmol for PCR; 8 mg/mmol for ACR). Source: NICE NG133 В§1.2.4вҖ“1.2.7.

instruction: Target BP when treating chronic or gestational hypertension or pre-eclampsia per NICE 2019?
output: Aim for BP of 135/85 mmHg or less on antihypertensive treatment, for chronic hypertension, gestational hypertension AND pre-eclampsia. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.3.9, Table 1, Table 2.

instruction: First-line antihypertensive in pregnancy per NICE NG133?
output: Labetalol вҖ” first choice in chronic hypertension, gestational hypertension and pre-eclampsia. If labetalol unsuitable, use nifedipine. If both unsuitable, use methyldopa. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.3.10, В§1.4.5, В§1.5.6.

instruction: When should methyldopa be stopped postpartum per NICE?
output: Stop methyldopa within 2 days after birth and change to an alternative antihypertensive (methyldopa is associated with postnatal depression). Source: NICE NG133 В§1.3.19, В§1.4.12, В§1.5.18.

instruction: Per NICE NG133, BP monitoring frequency for inpatient with severe hypertension?
output: Every 15 to 30 minutes until BP is less than 160/110 mmHg, then at least 4 times daily while the woman is an inpatient. Source: NICE NG133 Table 2.

instruction: When does NICE recommend planned early birth in chronic or gestational hypertension?
output: Do not offer planned early birth before 37 weeks for women whose BP is below 160/110 mmHg unless other medical indications. Beyond 37 weeks, timing should be jointly agreed with the senior obstetrician. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.3.14, В§1.4.7.

instruction: What is PLGF-based testing and when is it indicated per NICE?
output: Placental growth factor (PLGF)-based testing helps rule out pre-eclampsia in women with suspected pre-eclampsia (e.g., gestational hypertension) between 20 weeks and 36 weeks + 6 days. Carry out PLGF-based testing on 1 occasion. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.4.4, NICE DG23/DG49.

instruction: When should IV magnesium sulfate be given to women with severe pre-eclampsia per NICE?
output: Consider IV magnesium sulfate for women with severe pre-eclampsia who are in a critical care setting, if birth is planned within 24 hours. Loading dose 4 g IV over 5 to 15 min, then maintenance infusion 1 g per hour for 24 hours. If eclamptic fit recurs, give further 2-4 g IV over 5 min. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.8.2, В§1.8.4.

instruction: Per NICE NG133, BP target after the birth in women who had hypertension in pregnancy?
output: Aim to keep BP lower than 140/90 mmHg. Reduce antihypertensive treatment if BP falls below 130/80 mmHg. Offer review 2 weeks after birth and again 6 to 8 weeks postpartum. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.3.18, В§1.4.11вҖ“1.4.16.

instruction: Which antihypertensives are recommended for postnatal use including breastfeeding per NICE?
output: Enalapril (with renal monitoring) is first choice; if of Black African or Caribbean family origin, nifedipine (or amlodipine if previously used) is preferred; for combined choice, consider nifedipine OR amlodipine combined with enalapril. Atenolol or labetalol may be added or used to replace 1 of the agents. Avoid diuretics or angiotensin receptor blockers while breastfeeding. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.9.

instruction: Which moderate risk factors trigger aspirin per NICE NG133?
output: Two or more moderate risk factors: nulliparity, age 40 or older, pregnancy interval > 10 years, BMI вүҘ 35 kg/mВІ at booking, family history of pre-eclampsia, multi-fetal pregnancy. Start aspirin 75вҖ“150 mg from 12 weeks. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.1.3.

instruction: Should bed rest in hospital be offered as treatment for gestational hypertension per NICE?
output: No. NICE NG133 В§1.4.6 explicitly says: do not offer bed rest in hospital as a treatment for gestational hypertension. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.4.6.

instruction: What does NICE say about salt restriction to prevent pre-eclampsia?
output: Do not recommend salt restriction during pregnancy solely to prevent gestational hypertension or pre-eclampsia. Source: NICE NG133 В§1.1.6.

instruction: Diagnostic threshold for gestational diabetes per NICE NG3 (75g OGTT)?
output: Diagnose GDM if fasting plasma glucose вүҘ 5.6 mmol/L OR 2-hour plasma glucose вүҘ 7.8 mmol/L on 75 g OGTT. Test at 24вҖ“28 weeks (or sooner for previous GDM). Source: NICE NG3 В§1.2.5, В§1.2.8.

instruction: Capillary plasma glucose targets in pregnant women with any form of diabetes per NICE NG3?
output: Fasting: below 5.3 mmol/L; 1 hour after meals: below 7.8 mmol/L (or 2 hours after meals: below 6.4 mmol/L). Women on insulin should keep glucose above 4 mmol/L to avoid hypoglycaemia. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.3.5, В§1.3.6.

instruction: Preconception HbA1c targets and contraindications to conception per NICE NG3?
output: Aim for HbA1c below 48 mmol/mol (6.5%) before conception, if achievable without disabling hypoglycaemia. Strongly advise against conception if HbA1c above 86 mmol/mol (10%). Folic acid 5 mg/day until 12 weeks. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.1.18вҖ“1.1.20, В§1.1.11.

instruction: Which diabetes drugs can be used in pregnancy per NICE NG3?
output: Allowed: insulin (all preparations including rapid-acting analogues aspart and lispro), metformin (off-label but recommended as adjunct or alternative to insulin), isophane (NPH) as first-choice long-acting insulin. Stop before/at conception: all other oral hypoglycaemic agents, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, statins. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.1.21вҖ“1.1.25.

instruction: First-line treatment if GDM glucose targets not met with diet and exercise after 1-2 weeks?
output: Offer metformin. If contraindicated or unacceptable, offer insulin. If targets still not met on metformin + lifestyle, add insulin. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.2.19вҖ“1.2.21.

instruction: Per NICE NG3, when should women with GDM and fasting glucose вүҘ 7.0 mmol/L start insulin?
output: Offer immediate treatment with insulin, with or without metformin, plus diet and exercise changes. For fasting 6.0вҖ“6.9 mmol/L with complications (macrosomia or hydramnios) consider immediate insulin. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.2.22, В§1.2.23.

instruction: Timing of birth in women with type 1 or 2 diabetes per NICE NG3?
output: Elective birth by induction (or CS if indicated) between 37+0 and 38+6 weeks. Consider earlier if metabolic or maternal/fetal complications. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.4.2, В§1.4.3.

instruction: Timing of birth in uncomplicated gestational diabetes per NICE NG3?
output: Give birth no later than 40+6 weeks. Offer elective birth by induction (or CS if indicated) if not given birth by then. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.4.4.

instruction: Intrapartum capillary plasma glucose target for women with diabetes per NICE NG3?
output: Maintain capillary plasma glucose between 4 mmol/L and 7 mmol/L during labour and birth. Monitor every hour. Use IV dextrose-insulin infusion if glucose falls outside this range. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.4.10вҖ“1.4.12.

instruction: What neonatal blood glucose monitoring is required for babies of diabetic mothers per NICE NG3?
output: Blood glucose test 2 to 4 hours after birth. Feed within 30 minutes of birth, then every 2вҖ“3 hours until pre-feed glucose is maintained вүҘ 2.0 mmol/L. Additional intervention (tube feeding or IV dextrose) only if glucose < 2.0 mmol/L on 2 consecutive readings despite feeding, abnormal clinical signs, or unable to feed. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.5.3, В§1.5.9, В§1.5.10.

instruction: Postnatal diabetes testing for women who had GDM per NICE NG3?
output: Test glucose before transfer to community care. Offer fasting plasma glucose 6вҖ“13 weeks after birth (or HbA1c after 13 weeks if FPG missed). Do not routinely offer 75g 2-hour OGTT postnatally. Annual HbA1c thereafter if negative. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.6.11вҖ“1.6.14.

instruction: Per NICE NG3, when is rtCGM offered to pregnant women with type 1 diabetes?
output: Offer real-time continuous glucose monitoring (rtCGM) to ALL pregnant women with type 1 diabetes вҖ” proven to improve target achievement and neonatal outcomes. Offer isCGM ('flash') if rtCGM cannot be used or preferred. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.3.17, В§1.3.18.

instruction: When should women with type 1 diabetes test for ketonaemia per NICE NG3?
output: If they become hyperglycaemic or unwell, test for ketonaemia urgently and seek medical advice. Offer ketone testing strips and meter. Admit immediately for level 2 critical care if DKA suspected. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.3.21вҖ“1.3.24.

instruction: Retinal assessment schedule for women with pre-existing diabetes during pregnancy per NICE NG3?
output: Retinal assessment by digital imaging at first antenatal clinic visit (if none in last 3 months). If diabetic retinopathy, repeat at 16вҖ“20 weeks. Repeat for all at 28 weeks. Source: NICE NG3 В§1.3.25.

instruction: RCOG GTG 52 prophylactic uterotonic recommendation for vaginal birth?
output: Oxytocin 10 IU by intramuscular injection is the regimen of choice for active management of the third stage in women without PPH risk factors. Higher doses (40 or 80 IU) are not more effective. Source: RCOG GTG 52 В§4.1.

instruction: Prophylactic uterotonic for caesarean section per RCOG GTG 52?
output: Oxytocin 5 IU by slow intravenous injection. Higher rapid IV doses cause hypotension. Consider IV tranexamic acid 0.5вҖ“1.0 g additionally at CS for women at increased risk of PPH. Source: RCOG GTG 52 В§4.1, В§4.2.

instruction: When is ergometrineвҖ“oxytocin (Syntometrine) used per RCOG GTG 52?
output: ErgometrineвҖ“oxytocin may be used in absence of hypertension in women at increased risk of PPH. It reduces blood loss by ~80 ml compared with oxytocin alone but causes 5-fold more nausea, vomiting and BP elevation. CONTRAINDICATED in hypertension or pre-eclampsia. Source: RCOG GTG 52 В§4.1.

instruction: Order of pharmacological treatment of uterine atony per RCOG GTG 52?
output: Sequential drugs for atonic PPH: (1) oxytocin 5 IU slow IV (may repeat); (2) ergometrine 0.5 mg slow IV/IM вҖ” CI in HTN/PE; (3) oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 500 ml crystalloid at 125 ml/hr; (4) carboprost 0.25 mg IM, repeat at intervals вүҘ 15 min, max 8 doses; (5) misoprostol 800 micrograms sublingually. Source: RCOG GTG 52 В§5.3.3.

instruction: When should tranexamic acid be used in established PPH per WOMAN trial / RCOG?
output: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 min as soon as bleeding is recognised, repeat after 30 min if bleeding continues. WOMAN trial: most effective when given within 3 hours of onset; reduces death from bleeding by ~31% if given early. Source: RCOG GTG 52 В§5.3.4 / WOMAN trial 2017.

instruction: First-line surgical intervention for uterine atony unresponsive to drugs per RCOG GTG 52?
output: Intrauterine balloon tamponade вҖ” appropriate first-line 'surgical' intervention for most women in whom uterine atony is the cause of haemorrhage. Conservative surgical interventions (B-Lynch brace, uterine artery ligation) come second. Hysterectomy 'sooner rather than later' if placenta accreta or rupture. Source: RCOG GTG 52 В§7.3.

instruction: RCOG GTG 47 definition of anaemia in pregnancy?
output: First trimester Hb < 110 g/L; second/third trimester Hb < 105 g/L; postpartum Hb < 100 g/L. (BCSH thresholds adopted by RCOG.) Source: RCOG GTG 47 В§4.1.1.

instruction: When should pregnant women be screened for anaemia per RCOG GTG 47?
output: At booking and at 28 weeks. Women with multiple pregnancies have an additional FBC at 20вҖ“24 weeks. Source: RCOG GTG 47 В§4.1.1.

instruction: Approach to suspected iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy per RCOG GTG 47?
output: For normocytic or microcytic anaemia, trial oral iron first. Check Hb at 2 weeks вҖ” if no rise AND compliance confirmed, perform further tests (ferritin, haemoglobinopathy screen). Vitamin C improves absorption; tea/coffee inhibit. Source: RCOG GTG 47 В§4.1.1, В§4.1.2.

instruction: Indications for parenteral (IV) iron in pregnancy per RCOG GTG 47?
output: Oral iron not tolerated, not absorbed, compliance in doubt, OR approaching term with insufficient time for oral therapy. Administer only where staff trained for anaphylaxis are available. Source: RCOG GTG 47 В§4.1.2.

instruction: Red cell transfusion trigger for non-bleeding pregnant/postpartum women per RCOG GTG 47?
output: No firm criteria вҖ” decision is clinical. If Hb < 70 g/L with no active bleeding, consider transfusion (especially with cardiac/respiratory symptoms). Below 60 g/L, transfusion generally indicated. Always document indication and consent. Source: RCOG GTG 47 В§7.2.1, В§11.

instruction: Platelet transfusion trigger in major obstetric haemorrhage per RCOG GTG 47?
output: 75 Г— 10вҒ№/L during ongoing haemorrhage. Maintain a margin of safety to allow for further fall. Source: RCOG GTG 47 В§7.2.4.

instruction: Emergency blood for catastrophic haemorrhage when group unknown?
output: Group O RhD-negative AND K (Kell)-negative red cells should be available for immediate issue. Switch to group-specific or cross-matched units as soon as feasible. Source: RCOG GTG 47 В§5.3.

instruction: What advice should be given to women about fetal movements per RCOG GTG 57?
output: Advise women to report any decrease or cessation of fetal movements to their maternity provider. There is INSUFFICIENT evidence to recommend formal kick-counting using specified numbers (e.g., 'count to 10'). Encourage awareness of the pattern, not a number. Source: RCOG GTG 57 Recommendations 1вҖ“2.

instruction: Why is reduced fetal movement clinically significant per RCOG GTG 57?
output: Meta-analysis of 39 studies: women with RFM had increased odds of stillbirth (OR 3.44, 95% CI 2.02вҖ“5.88) and SGA infant. Association is also with fetal growth restriction, infection, and feto-maternal haemorrhage. Source: RCOG GTG 57 В§3.

instruction: Initial assessment of a woman presenting with RFM in community per RCOG GTG 57?
output: (1) Auscultate the fetal heart. (2) Take history. (3) Symphysis-fundal height measurement to assess size. (4) Arrange computerised CTG if вүҘ 26+0 weeks to exclude acute compromise. (5) USS within 24 hours if RFM persists despite normal CTG, has risk factors for FGR/stillbirth, or no USS in preceding 2 weeks. Source: RCOG GTG 57 Recommendations.

instruction: RFM before 24 weeks вҖ” what to do per RCOG GTG 57?
output: Confirm fetal heartbeat by handheld Doppler. RFM is much less commonly perceptible before 24 weeks; reassurance + 24-hr follow-up if heartbeat present. Source: RCOG GTG 57 В§Recommendations.

instruction: Recurrent RFM management per RCOG GTG 57?
output: Review case to exclude predisposing causes (FGR, placental insufficiency, infection, smoking, obesity, anterior placenta, polyhydramnios). Re-assess CTG and USS at each presentation. After 39+0 weeks with recurrent RFM, expediting birth does not appear to add risk. Source: RCOG GTG 57 В§Recommendations.

instruction: Should I attribute reduced fetal movements to maternal obesity or anterior placenta?
output: No вҖ” RFM should not be attributed to these factors. Obese women and those with anterior placenta may genuinely have reduced perception, but treating their RFM as 'normal for them' risks missing fetal compromise. Always investigate. Source: RCOG GTG 57.

instruction: When in multiple pregnancy does RFM warrant investigation per RCOG GTG 57?
output: Always вҖ” investigations should include CTG (for each fetus where possible), assessment of fetal growth, liquor volume, and umbilical artery Doppler. Source: RCOG GTG 57 В§Recommendations.

instruction: When should the first antenatal (booking) appointment take place per NICE NG201?
output: By 10+0 weeks of pregnancy. If a woman contacts maternity services after 9+0 weeks, the booking appointment should take place within 2 weeks. Source: NICE NG201 В§1.1.4вҖ“1.1.6.

instruction: How many antenatal appointments does NICE NG201 plan for nulliparous and parous women?
output: 10 routine appointments for nulliparous women, 7 for parous women. Additional or longer appointments offered based on need. Source: NICE NG201 В§1.1.7вҖ“1.1.10.

instruction: When should symphysis-fundal height be measured per NICE NG201?
output: At each antenatal appointment after 24+0 weeks. If concerns about small or large for gestational age, refer for assessment. Source: NICE NG201 В§1.2.30.

instruction: What does NICE NG201 advise about maternal sleep position?
output: After 28 weeks, advise women to avoid going to sleep on their back. Use pillows to maintain side-lying position. Evidence links supine sleep position to increased stillbirth risk in late pregnancy. Source: NICE NG201 В§1.3.25вҖ“1.3.26.

instruction: First-line treatment for nausea and vomiting in pregnancy per NICE NG201?
output: Reassure that mild-moderate nausea and vomiting are common and resolve by 16вҖ“20 weeks. Non-pharmacological: ginger. If pharmacological treatment chosen, offer an antiemetic (cyclizine, promethazine, prochlorperazine, ondansetron). IV fluids for moderate-severe nausea (preferably outpatient). Source: NICE NG201 В§1.4.1вҖ“1.4.7.

instruction: When is anti-D immunoglobulin offered for vaginal bleeding after 13 weeks per NICE NG201?
output: Offer anti-D Ig to women who present with vaginal bleeding after 13 weeks if they are rhesus D-negative AND at risk of isoimmunisation. Also refer to secondary care for review. Source: NICE NG201 В§1.4.16вҖ“1.4.17.

instruction: Heartburn in pregnancy first-line treatment per NICE NG201?
output: Lifestyle and dietary advice first. Then trial of an antacid or alginate. H2-receptor antagonists / PPIs only if simple measures fail. Source: NICE NG201 В§1.4.8вҖ“1.4.9.

instruction: Treatment of suspected vaginal candidiasis in pregnancy per NICE NG201?
output: Offer vaginal imidazole (such as clotrimazole or econazole). Oral azoles (e.g., fluconazole) generally avoided in pregnancy. Source: NICE NG201 В§1.4.13.

instruction: Pelvic girdle pain in pregnancy вҖ” NICE NG201 advice?
output: Consider referral to physiotherapy services for exercise advice and/or a non-rigid lumbopelvic belt. Source: NICE NG201 В§1.4.15.

output: Shoulder dystocia management - HELPERR: H - Help (call senior, paeds, anaesthetist). E - Evaluate for episiotomy. L - Legs - McRoberts manoeuvre (knees to chest, flexion-abduction). P - Suprapubic pressure (Mazzanti or Rubin I). E - Enter - internal manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw, reverse Woods). R - Remove posterior arm. R - Roll over (all-fours, Gaskin manoeuvre). Last resort - Zavanelli (cephalic replacement, CS), symphysiotomy. AVOID fundal pressure. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: RCOG GTG 42.

instruction: Pregnancy record (CHN form) - а·Җа·җа¶Ҝа¶ңа¶ӯа·Ҡ а¶ҡа¶»а·”а¶«а·”?
output: Sri Lanka CHN (Child Health Card) - mother retains, every visit update: (1) Demographics, parity, LMP, EDD. (2) Vital signs each visit. (3) Symphysis-fundal height, fetal heart. (4) Investigations + results. (5) Risk factors flagged. (6) Iron/folate adherence. (7) Immunisations. (8) Birth preparedness plan. (9) Postnatal contacts. (10) Newborn weight, immunisation. Safety plan - safe house, emergency contacts, escape kit. Refer: Mithuru Piyasa (govt centre), Women in Need (1938), 119 (police), social services. Confidentiality + woman's autonomy. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: WHO / Sri Lanka FHB.

instruction: а¶ңа¶»а·Ҡа¶·а¶«а·“ а¶ҡа·Ҹа¶Ҫа¶әа·ҡ stress а·ға·„ mental health а·ға¶ӯа·Ҡа¶ҡа·Ҹа¶»а¶ә?
output: ANC routine mental health screen: 4Q (Whooley + GAD-2). Risk factors: previous mental illness, single, young, GBV, no support, GDM/HTN, IUFD history. Mild: counselling, peer support, mindfulness. Moderate: CBT, group therapy. Severe: psychiatric referral, medication consideration (SSRIs - sertraline preferred breastfeeding). Suicidal ideation - urgent psychiatric, never leave alone. PHM 6-week postnatal EPDS screening. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: NICE CG192.

instruction: а¶ңа¶»а·Ҡа¶·а¶«а·“ а¶ҡа·Ҹа¶ұа·Ҡа¶ӯа·Ҹа·Җа¶ҡа·Ҡ а¶»а·”. 25 BMI, age 41, first pregnancy. а¶…а·Җа¶Ҝа·Ҹа¶ұа¶ёа·Ҡ а¶ёа·ңа¶ұа·Җа·Ҹа¶Ҝ?
output: Multiple moderate risk factors: nulliparity, age вүҘ40, BMI 25 (not high but combined). Risks: pre-eclampsia (start aspirin 75-150mg from 12 weeks), GDM (early OGTT consider), chromosomal abnormality (NIPT/combined test), stillbirth, IUGR (serial growth scans). Additional ANC visits. Birth - consider induction by 41 weeks. Continuous monitoring labour. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: NICE NG133, NG201.

instruction: First pregnancy, BMI 36, gestation 12 weeks. а¶ҡа·ҠвҖҚа¶»а·’а¶әа·Ҹ?
output: Obesity (BMI вүҘ30): (1) Aspirin 75-150mg from 12 weeks (PE prevention). (2) Folic acid 5mg daily (NTD increased risk). (3) Vitamin D supplementation. (4) GDM screen at 24-28 weeks (early if other risks). (5) Anomaly scan technical difficulties counsel. (6) Anaesthetic referral 3rd trimester. (7) Birth at consultant-led unit. (8) VTE assessment - LMWH antenatal if high risk. (9) Refer dietitian. (10) Postnatal weight management. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: RCOG / NICE.

instruction: а¶ңа¶»а·Ҡа¶·а¶«а·“ а¶ҡа·Ҹа¶ұа·Ҡа¶ӯа·Ҹа·Җа¶ҡа·Ҡ pre-existing HTN, captopril 25mg а¶ңа¶ұа·Ҡа¶ұ. а¶Ҝа·җа¶ұа·Ҡ а¶ҡа¶»а¶ұа·Ҡа¶ұа·ҡ?
output: ACE inhibitor teratogenic - stop immediately, switch within 2 working days. Alternatives: (1) Labetalol (first choice). (2) Nifedipine. (3) Methyldopa. Target BP 135/85. Aspirin 75-150mg from 12 weeks (chronic HTN = high-risk for PE). PLGF testing 20-36+6 weeks if PE suspected. Increased ANC visits. Renal function baseline. Birth: typically 37-39 weeks unless BP вүҘ 160/110 or fetal indication. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: NICE NG133.

instruction: Mastitis - а¶ҡа·ҠвҖҚа¶»а·’а¶әа·Ҹ?
output: Breast pain, redness, fever, flu-like symptoms - mastitis. (1) Continue breastfeeding (more frequent on affected side). (2) Effective milk removal - good latch, massage. (3) Warm compress before feeds, cold after. (4) Paracetamol/ibuprofen pain. (5) Rest, hydration. (6) Antibiotics if symptoms persist 12-24h, fever вүҘ38.5, severe - flucloxacillin 500mg qid 10-14 days (or erythromycin if penicillin-allergic). (7) Abscess (fluctuant mass) - USS, drainage. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: BFHI / RCOG.

instruction: а¶ҙа·ҠвҖҚа¶»а·ға·–а¶ӯа·’а¶әа·ҷа¶ұа·Ҡ а¶ҙа·ға·” urine leak а·Җа·ҷа¶әа·’. а·ға·Ҹа¶ёа·Ҹа¶ұа·ҠвҖҚа¶әа¶Ҝ?
output: Postpartum stress urinary incontinence - pelvic floor injury а·„а·ҡа¶ӯа·”а·Җ. 6 а·ға¶ӯа·’ а¶ҙа¶ёа¶« а·ға·Ҹа¶ёа·Ҹа¶ұа·ҠвҖҚа¶ә. а¶ҡа·ҠвҖҚа¶»а·’а¶әа·Ҹ: (1) Pelvic floor exercises (Kegel) - а¶ҙа·җа¶ә а·„а·җа¶ё а¶ҙа·җа¶әа¶ҡа¶ё 10 contractions Г— 10 seconds, 3 sets/day. (2) Bladder training. (3) 6 а·ға¶ӯа·’ а¶ҙа·ға·” - physiotherapy referral. (4) Severe (continuous leak, fistula) - urological referral. CS women too can have leak. Persistent >6 months - specialist evaluation. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: NICE.

instruction: а¶ҙа·ҠвҖҚа¶»а·ға·–а¶ӯа·’а¶әа·ҷа¶ұа·Ҡ а¶ҙа·ға·” constipation а·ға·„ haemorrhoids?
output: Postpartum common - opioid analgesia, dehydration, fear of pain. а¶ҡа·ҠвҖҚа¶»а·’а¶әа·Ҹ: (1) Fluids 2-3L/day. (2) Fiber - papaya, fruits, vegetables. (3) Lactulose 10-15ml bd safe in breastfeeding. (4) Avoid straining - perineal pain worse. (5) Haemorrhoids - topical anaesthetic (lignocaine), warm sitz baths, witch hazel pads, ice packs. (6) Persistent (>6 weeks), bleeding heavy, prolapsed - surgical referral. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: NICE postnatal.

instruction: Mother calls in night: contractions 10 minutes apart, gestation 39 weeks. Advice?
output: Latent phase likely. (1) Stay home if comfortable, rest, eat light, hydrate. (2) Come to hospital when: contractions 5 min apart for 1 hour AND lasting 60 sec each, OR ROM, OR bleeding more than show, OR reduced fetal movements, OR severe pain. (3) Always come if anxious or worried. (4) High risk (HTN, GDM, scar) - lower threshold to come in. (5) Bring CHN card, supplies. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: MOH 2013.

instruction: PHM home visit: postnatal day 5, mother BP 155/100, severe headache. Action?

(4) а·Җа·„а·Ҹа¶ё transport - hospital - ambulance call 1990. (5) Do NOT give methyldopa (depression). (6) Hospital: IV access, MgSO4 consideration, antihypertensives. (7) Bloods - FBC, LFT, U&E. Postnatal PE accounts for ~40% of severe maternal morbidity. а¶ёа·–а¶Ҫа·Ҹа·Ға·ҠвҖҚа¶»а¶ә: NICE NG133 / MBRRACE.

instruction: а®•а®°аҜҚа®ӘаҜҚа®Әа®ҝа®Ја®ҝа®ӨаҜҚ а®Өа®ҫа®Ҝа®ҝа®©аҜҚ а®Ҷа®Әа®ӨаҜҚа®ӨаҜҒ а®…а®ұа®ҝа®•аҜҒа®ұа®ҝа®•а®іаҜҚ а®Һа®©аҜҚа®©аҜҶа®©аҜҚа®©?
output: а®үа®ҹа®©а®ҹа®ҝа®Ҝа®ҫа®• а®®а®°аҜҒа®ӨаҜҚа®ӨаҜҒа®өа®®а®©аҜҲа®•аҜҚа®•аҜҒа®ҡаҜҚ а®ҡаҜҶа®ІаҜҚа®Іа®өаҜҒа®®аҜҚ: а®•а®ҹаҜҒа®®аҜҲа®Ҝа®ҫа®© а®Өа®ІаҜҲа®өа®Іа®ҝ, а®Әа®ҫа®°аҜҚа®өаҜҲ а®®а®ҷаҜҚа®•а®ІаҜҚ а®…а®ІаҜҚа®Іа®ӨаҜҒ а®®а®ҝа®©аҜҒа®®а®ҝа®©аҜҒа®ӘаҜҚа®ӘаҜҒ, а®®аҜҒа®•а®®аҜҚ а®®а®ұаҜҚа®ұаҜҒа®®аҜҚ а®•аҜҲа®•а®іаҜҚ а®өаҜҖа®•аҜҚа®•а®®аҜҚ, а®өа®Ҝа®ҝа®ұаҜҚа®ұа®ҝа®©аҜҚ а®®аҜҮа®ІаҜҚ а®өа®Іа®ӨаҜҒа®ӘаҜҒа®ұа®®аҜҚ а®•а®ҹаҜҒа®®аҜҲа®Ҝа®ҫа®© а®өа®Іа®ҝ, а®ҜаҜӢа®©а®ҝ а®өа®ҙа®ҝа®Ҝа®ҫа®• а®Үа®°а®ӨаҜҚа®Өа®ӘаҜҚа®ӘаҜӢа®•аҜҚа®•аҜҒ, а®’а®°аҜҒ а®Ёа®ҫа®іаҜҒа®•аҜҚа®•аҜҒ а®®аҜҮа®Іа®ҫа®• а®•аҜҒа®ҙа®ЁаҜҚа®ӨаҜҲа®Ҝа®ҝа®©аҜҚ а®…а®ҡаҜҲа®өаҜҒ а®Үа®ІаҜҚа®Іа®ҫа®®аҜҲ, а®…а®Өа®ҝа®• а®•а®ҫа®ҜаҜҚа®ҡаҜҚа®ҡа®ІаҜҚ, а®ҡа®ҝа®ұаҜҒа®ЁаҜҖа®°аҜҚ а®•а®ҙа®ҝа®•аҜҚа®•аҜҲа®Ҝа®ҝа®ІаҜҚ а®өа®Іа®ҝ, а®“а®ҜаҜҚа®өа®ҝа®ІаҜҚ а®®аҜӮа®ҡаҜҚа®ҡаҜҒа®ӨаҜҚ а®Өа®ҝа®Ја®ұа®ІаҜҚ. а®®аҜӮа®Іа®®аҜҚ: WHO ANC / RCOG / PHM а®Әа®Ҝа®ҝа®ұаҜҚа®ҡа®ҝ.

instruction: а®Һа®©а®ӨаҜҒ а®Үа®°а®ӨаҜҚа®Ө а®…а®ҙаҜҒа®ӨаҜҚа®Өа®®аҜҚ 162/108. а®өа®Ҝа®ҝа®ұаҜҚа®ұа®ҝа®©аҜҚ а®®аҜҮа®ІаҜҚ а®өа®Іа®ӨаҜҒа®ӘаҜҒа®ұа®®аҜҚ а®өа®Іа®ҝ а®үа®іаҜҚа®іа®ӨаҜҒ. а®Һа®©аҜҚ а®Ёа®ҝа®ІаҜҲ а®Һа®©аҜҚа®©?

BP 160/110-а®җ а®өа®ҝа®ҹ а®…а®Өа®ҝа®•а®®аҜҚ + а®өа®Ҝа®ҝа®ұаҜҚа®ұаҜҒ а®өа®Іа®ҝ а®Ёа®°а®®аҜҚа®Әа®ҝа®Ҝа®ІаҜҚ а®…а®өа®ҡа®° а®Ёа®ҝа®ІаҜҲа®Ҝа®ҝа®©аҜҚ а®…а®ұа®ҝа®•аҜҒа®ұа®ҝ. а®Өа®ҫа®®а®Өа®ҝа®•аҜҚа®• а®өаҜҮа®ЈаҜҚа®ҹа®ҫа®®аҜҚ. а®®аҜӮа®Іа®®аҜҚ: NICE NG133 / MOH 2013.

instruction: а®Ёа®ҫа®©аҜҚ 7а®өа®ӨаҜҒ а®®а®ҫа®Өа®®аҜҚ а®•а®°аҜҚа®ӘаҜҚа®Әа®®аҜҚ. а®•аҜҒа®ҙа®ЁаҜҚа®ӨаҜҲа®Ҝа®ҝа®©аҜҚ а®…а®ҡаҜҲа®өаҜҒ а®•аҜҒа®ұаҜҲа®өа®ҫа®• а®үа®іаҜҚа®іа®ӨаҜҒ. а®Һа®©аҜҚа®© а®ҡаҜҶа®ҜаҜҚа®өа®ӨаҜҒ?
